From: "McCarroll, James C." <JMcCarroll@ReedSmith.com>
Subject: FW: Our Retainer
Date: Fri, 26 Oct 2007 16:36:16 GMT
To: dkiesq@aol.com

James C. McCarroll 
jmccarroll@reedsmith.com 
Reed Smith LLP
Direct Tel.:  212-549-0209 
Main Line:  212-521-5400 
Facsimile:  212-521-5450 
599 Lexington Avenue
New York, New York  10022
View Bio
<http://www.reedsmith.com/our_people.cfm?cit_id=2254&widCall1=customWidg

________________________________

From: Harvey Pitt [mailto:Harvey@kaloramapartners.com] 
Sent: Thursday, October 25, 2007 6:21 PM
To: Chris Meyering; McCarroll, James C.
Cc: John Sampson
Subject: Our Retainer
Importance: High


Dear Chris and Jim,
 
We're scheduled to meet in NY tomorrow, but still haven't received the
first tranche of our retainer payment.  Moreover, I don't know who our
purported clients are, and whether they've actually agreed to pay our
fees.  Also, I learned yesterday that, at a meeting I understand Chris
attended, Dilenschneider Group was told (by Panos I think) that it
wouldn't receive payment for its efforts to date, which is quite
troubling both because they are important to our efforts and also
because of what it reflects about the willingness of this group to pay
our fees as agreed.
 
It's one thing to put my personal reputation on the line by making
telephone calls, which I've done.  It's another to start expending our
own funds when we haven't been paid according to our retainer agreement.
There's an obligation to reimburse us for travel, but considering that
we haven't even gotten our promised fees first, undertaking this expense
seems foolish.
 
The failure to receive our agreed-upon payment, and the information
regarding Dilenschneider, leaves us queasy.  I'm writing this because
we'd like some comfort that we haven't been given a worthless promise.
If it's this difficult to get payment at the outset of our relationship,
what reason is there to believe it will get any easier to receive
further promised payments and out-of-pocket travel expenses?
 
I'm scheduled to be on a 10 am train to New York tomorrow, for our
meeting.  I'm seriously wondering whether we want to do business this
way.  We can cut our losses now, and avoid further embarrassment as well
as expense.  Or, we can get the money we've been told will come, and
have reasonable assurances of the remainder being paid, and go forward.
 
Tell me why, under these circumstances, I should board my train
tomorrow.  Also, tell me why I should believe that this won't keep
happening for the duration of our relationship.
 
Harvey
 
 
 
Harvey L. Pitt
Kalorama Partners, LLC
1130 Connecticut Ave., NW
Suite 800
Washington, DC 20036-3915
Phone:          (202) 721-0000
Fax:                (202) 721-0007
Direct Fax: (202) 721-0008
Cell:          (202) 321-5045
email:      harvey@kaloramapartners.com
<mailto:harvey@kaloramapartners.com> 
website: www.kaloramapartners.com <http://www.kaloramapartners.com/> 
 
* * *

Thank you for your cooperation.

From: Harvey Pitt [mailto:Harvey@kaloramapartners.com]
Sent: Thursday, October 25, 2007 6:21 PM
To: Chris Meyering; McCarroll, James C.
Cc: John Sampson
Subject: Our Retainer
Importance: High

Dear Chris and Jim,
 
We're scheduled to meet in NY tomorrow, but still haven't received the first tranche of our retainer payment.  Moreover, I don't know who our purported clients are, and whether they've actually agreed to pay our fees.  Also, I learned yesterday that, at a meeting I understand Chris attended, Dilenschneider Group was told (by Panos I think) that it wouldn't receive payment for its efforts to date, which is quite troubling both because they are important to our efforts and also because of what it reflects about the willingness of this group to pay our fees as agreed.
 
It's one thing to put my personal reputation on the line by making telephone calls, which I've done.  It's another to start expending our own funds when we haven't been paid according to our retainer agreement.  There's an obligation to reimburse us for travel, but considering that we haven't even gotten our promised fees first, undertaking this expense seems foolish.
 
The failure to receive our agreed-upon payment, and the information regarding Dilenschneider, leaves us queasy.  I'm writing this because we'd like some comfort that we haven't been given a worthless promise.  If it's this difficult to get payment at the outset of our relationship, what reason is there to believe it will get any easier to receive further promised payments and out-of-pocket travel expenses?
 
I'm scheduled to be on a 10 am train to New York tomorrow, for our meeting.  I'm seriously wondering whether we want to do business this way.  We can cut our losses now, and avoid further embarrassment as well as expense.  Or, we can get the money we've been told will come, and have reasonable assurances of the remainder being paid, and go forward.
 
Tell me why, under these circumstances, I should board my train tomorrow.  Also, tell me why I should believe that this won't keep happening for the duration of our relationship.
 
Harvey
